Description: Do brisk walking, jogging, cycling, swimming, or similar moderate-to-vigorous movement for 20–45 min on most days.
Summary: Network MA, 218 RCTs / 14,170 participants; walking-jogging reduced depression vs active controls (g −0.62), effect grows with intensity.
Claude Reasoning: Highest evidence form (network MA of RCTs) at the largest scale available, and the benefit holds against active controls — a clinically meaningful effect that clears the bar for the top tier.

Description: Do brisk walking, jogging, cycling, swimming, or similar moderate-to-vigorous movement for 20–45 min on most days.
Paper: Leung et al., 2025, Br. J. Sports Medicine
Summary: SR/MA, 36 RCTs / 2,500 patients with chronic illness + comorbid depression; aerobic exercise g −0.73, with dose-response.
Claude Reasoning: Independent replication in a harder-to-treat population with a moderate-large effect and a dose gradient — shows the benefit generalizes, which is what a Strong ranking requires.

Description: Do brisk walking, jogging, cycling, swimming, or similar moderate-to-vigorous movement for 20–45 min on most days.
Paper: Du et al., 2026, BMC Public Health
Summary: Bayesian dose-response MA, 12 RCTs / 723 adolescents; g −0.28, benefit peaking ~740 MET-min/week.
Claude Reasoning: A third independent MA in a different age group confirms a significant effect and dose-response — cross-population consistency reinforces the robustness expected at the top tier.

Description: Focus on breath, body, or sound, and gently return attention whenever the mind wanders.
Paper: Goyal et al., 2014, JAMA Internal Medicine
Summary: SR/MA of 47 active-control RCTs / 3,515 participants; moderate evidence mindfulness meditation improves anxiety (0.38), depression (0.30), pain.
Claude Reasoning: The most conservative possible test — only active-control RCTs, which strip out placebo/expectancy — still finds durable benefit. Surviving that bar is the strongest justification for a Strong ranking.
ChatGPT Critique: The ranking is defensible, but the reasoning is a little too strong. Goyal et al. found small-to-moderate benefits against time/attention active controls, but also found no evidence that meditation beat specific active treatments such as exercise, drugs, or behavioral therapies; phrases like “most conservative possible test” and “durable benefit” should be softened.

Description: Focus on breath, body, or sound, and gently return attention whenever the mind wanders.
Paper: Galante et al., 2023, Nature Mental Health
Summary: Individual-participant-data MA, 13 RCTs / 2,371 participants; distress SMD −0.32 at 1–6 months; confidence rated "high."
Claude Reasoning: IPD meta-analysis is the gold-standard synthesis form, and the authors themselves grade confidence as high — top-tier methodological pedigree behind the ranking.
ChatGPT Critique: Strong synthesis method, but the comparison was passive/no-intervention control in non-clinical volunteers, not active control. It supports mindfulness versus doing nothing more than a specific effect beyond expectancy, time, or attention.

Description: Focus on breath, body, or sound, and gently return attention whenever the mind wanders.
Summary: SR/MA, 136 RCTs / 11,605 participants; vs no intervention depression SMD −0.53, anxiety −0.56.
Claude Reasoning: Establishes breadth (136 RCTs) of benefit; the fact that effects shrink vs active controls is exactly why the ranking is Strong rather than over-stated, so it supports the placement honestly.

Description: Make the good option the default and add extra steps to the bad option.
Paper: Jachimowicz et al., 2019, Behavioural Public Policy
Summary: MA of 58 default studies / n=73,675 (on-target to defaults); d=0.68, with honest variability (a few null, two negative).
Claude Reasoning: Construct-matched MA with a large average effect but real heterogeneity (some null/negative). That combination — strong but not universal — is precisely what a Moderate-to-Strong (not Strong) ranking should reflect.

Description: Make the good option the default and add extra steps to the bad option.
Summary: MA, 200+ studies / n≈2.1M; choice architecture d=0.43; decision-structure nudges (incl. defaults) strongest category.
Claude Reasoning: Huge cross-domain MA confirms the broader family works and that defaults are its strongest sub-class — situating the technique among the better-evidenced behavioral tools, consistent with a high-but-not-top ranking.
ChatGPT Critique: Useful supporting evidence for choice architecture generally, but this is not defaults-specific evidence. Also, the PubMed/PNAS record reports publication in 2022, more than 450 effect sizes, n = 2,149,683, and d = 0.45, so the row’s 2021/d = 0.43 wording is slightly off.

Description: Make the good option the default and add extra steps to the bad option.
Summary: Classic opt-in vs opt-out organ-donation studies showing how large a participation shift a default produces.
Claude Reasoning: Demonstrates mechanism and magnitude in a high-stakes real-world setting, evidencing external validity — corroborates that defaults reliably move behavior.
ChatGPT Critique: This paper is mainly about the psychological meaning/mechanism of opt-in versus opt-out organ-donation defaults. It notes that defaults strongly affect participation, but its own studies do not directly estimate real-world participation shifts; the reasoning overstates it as direct high-stakes behavioral magnitude/external-validity evidence.
Gemini Critique: The reasoning incorrectly attributes the demonstration of real-world magnitude of default effects to Davidai et al., 2012. The classic paper demonstrating the massive participation shift in real-world organ donation is actually Johnson & Goldstein (2003) in Science. Davidai et al. (2012) explores the psychological *meaning* and construal behind these policies (e.g., why they work and how people perceive the sacrifice), rather than demonstrating the behavioral shift itself. While the paper is relevant to the topic of defaults, the summary and reasoning describe the findings of the wrong paper.

Description: Mentally rehearse the steps, obstacles, and responses rather than only the desired outcome.
Paper: Lindsay et al., 2021, Physical Education & Sport Pedagogy
Summary: SR/MA, 36 studies / 1,449 participants; mental imagery improves performance g=0.476 (imagery+physical 0.579; imagery alone 0.298).
Claude Reasoning: Construct-matched to process rehearsal (motor imagery, not outcome fantasy) with a moderate effect on real performance — direct, on-target support justifying a strong-but-not-top tier.
ChatGPT Critique: The paper is on sport-specific motor imagery, not the broader self-help technique of rehearsing steps, obstacles, and responses for general goals. It directly supports motor-skill mental practice, but only indirectly supports “process visualization”; the ranking is too broad for this evidence alone.

Description: Mentally rehearse the steps, obstacles, and responses rather than only the desired outcome.
Paper: Toth et al., 2020, Psychology of Sport & Exercise
Summary: Methodological replication of Driskell (1994); 37 studies; small but robust positive effect (r=0.131) after publication-bias correction.
Claude Reasoning: A deliberately skeptical, bias-corrected replication still finds a reliable effect; the modest residual size is exactly why Moderate-to-Strong rather than Strong is the honest call.
ChatGPT Critique: Good skeptical evidence that mental practice has a small residual performance effect, but it is still a mental-practice/performance literature, not a direct test of general goal-process visualization or obstacle-response planning. It supports a narrower claim than the technique description.

Description: Mentally rehearse the steps, obstacles, and responses rather than only the desired outcome.
Paper: Simonsmeier et al., 2020, Int. Review of Sport & Exercise Psychology
Summary: MA of imagery interventions; medium effect d=0.431; imagery + physical practice beats physical alone.
Claude Reasoning: Independent MA reaches a similar medium effect and shows imagery adds value over physical practice alone — convergent evidence across reviews supports the ranking.
ChatGPT Critique: This is again sport/imagery intervention evidence. “Imagery plus physical practice beats physical practice alone” is relevant to athletic or motor rehearsal, but not enough to establish broad self-help process visualization as Moderate-to-Strong.

Description: Stand or sit expansively for a minute or two before a challenging interaction.
Paper: Elkjær et al., 2020, Perspectives on Psychological Science
Summary: MA, 73 studies; effects driven by avoiding contractive postures (g=0.45), while the expansive "power pose" vs neutral ≈ g=0.06 (null).
Claude Reasoning: The largest MA shows the specific popular claim (adopt an expansive pose) is essentially null; any effect comes from not slumping. That downgrades it to a weak/uncertain signal — Questionable, but not Unsupported, since a postural effect of some kind is real.
ChatGPT Critique: The direction is right, but because the technique is specifically “stand or sit expansively,” the near-null expansive-versus-neutral result means the exact technique is closer to unsupported or very weak. The defensible advice is better framed as “avoid contractive/slumped posture,” not “power pose.”

Description: Stand or sit expansively for a minute or two before a challenging interaction.
Summary: P-curve of the 33 studies cited as supportive; distribution indistinguishable from no effect + selective reporting.
Claude Reasoning: Shows the original "it works" literature has no evidential value once selective reporting is accounted for — direct grounds for demoting the claim to Questionable.

Description: Stand or sit expansively for a minute or two before a challenging interaction.
Summary: Large (N=200), experimenter-blind replication: reproduced felt power but no effect on hormones or any behavioral task.
Claude Reasoning: The decisive high-powered replication: the subjective "feel powerful" effect survives but every consequential outcome fails — capturing the "weak signal, nothing actionable" character of the Questionable tier.

Description: Hold or display a crystal to balance energy or heal yourself.
Summary: RCT, 138 adults, rose quartz vs visually-matched fake crystal, 14-day protocol; no difference, Bayesian null; relief only among believers (expectancy). No second rigorous study exists — absence is the point.
Claude Reasoning: A controlled head-to-head of real vs fake crystal finds zero specific effect, with Bayesian evidence for the null and benefit explained entirely by placebo/belief — the textbook pattern for Unsupported. The lack of any rigorous study showing a genuine effect reinforces the ranking.
ChatGPT Critique: Unsupported is the right call for a specific crystal-healing effect, but “no second rigorous study exists” is broader than this paper itself establishes. The cited RCT is also limited to non-clinical anxiety/stress and placebo/expectancy effects, not all forms of “healing.”
